# Scan GitHub for secrets

Connect GitHub as a data source to HCP Vault Radar to scan your GitHub repositories
for sensitive data and secrets. Vault Radar supports scanning GitHub
Cloud and GitHub Enterprise Server. You can connect to GitHub either as a data source host 
to auto discover GitHub organizations or as a data source tied to a single GitHub organization.

@include 'vault-radar/radar-quickstart-tutorials.mdx'

<Note>

Vault Radar has a limit of 5000 repositories per connection. If you have more
than 5000 repositories, Vault Radar will select the 5000 repositories with the
most recent activity.

</Note>

## Security and operations considerations

You can add GitHub as a data source in HCP Vault Radar using either a GitHub App
or a personal access token (PAT). 

The GitHub App is the recommended authentication method because it uses
short-lived installation access tokens with scoped permissions, providing more
granular access control. Installation access tokens are well-suited for use
cases where long-lived user credentials are undesirable.

PAT authentication is easier to set up, but it uses long-lived tokens tied to a
specific GitHub user, which can pose security risks if the token is compromised.
PAT authentication also provides broader permissions, and has access to any
repositories the user has access to.

### Data source vs data source host

You can use GitHub App authentication with HCP Vault Radar data source. A data
source supports a single GitHub organization per connection. If you have
multiple GitHub organizations, you can add multiple data sources to Vault Radar,
one for each organization.

You can use PAT authentication as either a data source, or a data source host. A
data source host supports multiple GitHub organizations in a single connection.

This is an important security and operations trade-off to consider when deciding
which authentication method to use.

| Requirement                                  | GitHub App authentication | Personal access token (PAT) authentication |
|----------------------------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Single connection for multiple organizations | &#10060                   | &#9989;                                    |
| Connection per organization                  | &#9989;                   | &#9989;                                    |
| Short-lived tokens                           | &#9989;                   | &#10060;                                   |
| Limited repository access                    | &#9989;                   | &#10060;                                   |
| Organization-level governance                | &#9989;                   | &#10060;                                   |
| Independent identity                         | &#9989;                   | &#10060;                                   |
| Tied to a specific GitHub user               | &#10060;                  | &#9989;                                    |

## Prerequisites

- HCP IAM user with the HCP owner, or admin role
- [Vault Radar agent](/hcp/docs/vault-radar/agent/deploy) deployed and running (agent scan only)
- Python 3.10.20 or later installed (GitHub App authentication only)
- For GitHub App authentication with GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server,
  create a GitHub App.
- For GitHub [personal access token
  (PAT)](https://github.com/settings/tokens/new?scopes=repo,read:org,admin:repo_hook,admin:org_hook,read:user)
  authentication with GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server, create a PAT
  with the following permission scopes:

  <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

  ```plaintext
  repo > all
  admin:org > read:org
  admin:repo_hook > all
  admin:org_hook > all
  user > read:user
  ```

  </CodeBlockConfig>

## Add a GitHub data source with app authentication

You can add and scan GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server using the **HCP
Vault Radar cloud scan** or **HCP Vault Radar agent scan**.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="GitHub Cloud">

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar cloud scan">

Using app authentication with the HCP Vault Radar cloud scan does not require
Python 3.10.20 or later to complete the setup.

### Set up HCP Vault Radar

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data sources**.

1. Click **Add data source**.

1. Select **HCP Vault Radar Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Cloud**.

1. Under **Select authentication method**, click the dropdown and select **GitHub OAuth** to authenticate with the GitHub App.

1. Click **Connect to GitHub**.

1. On redirect to GitHub, select which GitHub Org to Onboard and the repositories to install on.

1. On successful redirect to **Vault Radar**, click **Next**.

1. Select either **All active repositories** or **Select repositories to monitor**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar agent scan">

### Set up GitHub app authentication

You must create and register a GitHub App before setting up HCP Vault Radar.

1. Download `githubchecks-app.gen.py`.

   ```python
   import logging
   import sys
   from secrets import token_urlsafe
   from urllib.parse import urlencode, urlparse
   
   def init_logger():
       logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
       logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
       h = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
       logger.addHandler(h)
   
       return logger
   
   logger = init_logger()
   
   if __name__ == '__main__':
       print("Please select an option GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server:")
       print("1. GitHub Cloud")
       print("2. GitHub Enterprise Server")
       choice = input("Enter 1 or 2: ")
   
       name = 'HashiCorp Vault Radar Checks App'
       domain = 'github.com'
       webhook_url =  'https://api.hashicorp.cloud/2023-05-01/vault-radar/api/github-apps/events'
       if choice == '1':
           name = input('Enter a unique GitHub app name: ')
       elif choice == '2':
           domain = input('Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name (i.e. github.companyname.com): ')
           domain = urlparse(domain if '//' in domain else f'//{domain}').netloc
           webhook_url = webhook_url + f"?domain={domain}"
       else:
           print("Invalid option")
           sys.exit()
   
       query_params = {
           'name': name,
           'webhook_secret': token_urlsafe(nbytes=32),
           'webhook_url': webhook_url,
           'public': True,
           'webhook_active': True,
           'url': 'https://www.hashicorp.com/',
           'checks': 'write',
           'events[]': ['check_run', 'check_suite', 'pull_request'],
           'pull_requests': 'read',
       }
       query_param_string = urlencode(query_params, doseq=True, safe=':/?=[]')
       
       url = f'https://{domain}/settings/apps/new?{query_param_string}'
       logger.info(url)
   ```

   You can view the source code for this script in the [HashiCorp guides GitHub
   repository](https://github.com/hashicorp-guides/vault-radar-demo/tree/main/github-app).

1. Run the script and follow the prompt.

   ```shell-session
   $ python3 github-checks-app-gen.py
   ```

1. When prompted, select GitHub Cloud by entering `1`.

1. Provide a name for your Vault Radar GitHub App.

1. Copy/paste the URL into a new browser window/tab.

1. Copy the `webhook_secret=webhook_secret` in the URL.

   **Example output:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name (i.e. github.companyname.com): acme.github.com
   https://acme.github.com...webhook_secret=Z1KPOYifctpzOjfphKj_hqRlZbrDOBG9AU7hgj7iPrk...
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>
   
1. Add the `webhook_secret` into the form.

1. Remove `?domain=github.com` from the Webhook URL.

1. Scroll down and click **Create GitHub App**.

1. Take note of the values from your app settings page:

   - Webhook secret from the URL above
   - **App name**
   - **App ID**
   - **Private key**

1. Save these values in a secure location, such as IBM Vault. 

1. [Configure an environment variable](/hcp/docs/vault-radar/agent/deploy) on
   the agent with the value of the private key.

   **Example configuration:** 

   If you run the agent on Kubernetes, add the environment
   variable to the agent deployment manifest.

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   env:
     - name: GH_RADAR_PRIVATE_KEY
       value: `string-of-your-private-key`
   ```
   
   </CodeBlockConfig>

   <Highlight>

   Use the same variable name on all agents.

   </Highlight>

   You are ready to add GitHub as a data source in HCP Vault Radar.

### Set up HCP Vault Radar

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data sources**.

1. Click **Add data source**.

1. Select **HCP Vault Radar Agent Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Cloud**.

1. Enter the path to the environment variable with the personal access token in
   the Enter environment path to your GitHub personal access token field.

   **Example:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   env://GH_RADAR_PRIVATE_KEY
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>

1. Under **Select authentication method**, click the dropdown and select **GitHub OAuth** to authenticate with the GitHub App.

1. Click **Connect to GitHub**.

1. On redirect to GitHub, select which GitHub Org to onboard and the repositories to install on.

1. On successful redirect to Vault Radar, click **Next**.

1. Select either **All active repositories** or **Select repositories to monitor**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

</Tab>
<Tab heading="GitHub Enterprise Server">

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar cloud scan">

### Set up GitHub app authentication

You must create and register a GitHub App before setting up HCP Vault Radar.

1. Download `githubchecks-app.gen.py`.

   ```python
   import logging
   import sys
   from secrets import token_urlsafe
   from urllib.parse import urlencode, urlparse
   
   def init_logger():
       logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
       logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
       h = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
       logger.addHandler(h)
   
       return logger
   
   logger = init_logger()
   
   if __name__ == '__main__':
       print("Please select an option GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server:")
       print("1. GitHub Cloud")
       print("2. GitHub Enterprise Server")
       choice = input("Enter 1 or 2: ")
   
       name = 'HashiCorp Vault Radar Checks App'
       domain = 'github.com'
       webhook_url =  'https://api.hashicorp.cloud/2023-05-01/vault-radar/api/github-apps/events'
       if choice == '1':
           name = input('Enter a unique GitHub app name: ')
       elif choice == '2':
           domain = input('Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name (i.e. github.companyname.com): ')
           domain = urlparse(domain if '//' in domain else f'//{domain}').netloc
           webhook_url = webhook_url + f"?domain={domain}"
       else:
           print("Invalid option")
           sys.exit()
   
       query_params = {
           'name': name,
           'webhook_secret': token_urlsafe(nbytes=32),
           'webhook_url': webhook_url,
           'public': True,
           'webhook_active': True,
           'url': 'https://www.hashicorp.com/',
           'checks': 'write',
           'events[]': ['check_run', 'check_suite', 'pull_request'],
           'pull_requests': 'read',
       }
       query_param_string = urlencode(query_params, doseq=True, safe=':/?=[]')
       
       url = f'https://{domain}/settings/apps/new?{query_param_string}'
       logger.info(url)
   ```

   You can view the source code for this script in the [HashiCorp guides GitHub
   repository](https://github.com/hashicorp-guides/vault-radar-demo/tree/main/github-app).

1. Run the script and follow the prompt.

   ```shell-session
   $ python3 github-checks-app-gen.py
   ```

1. When prompted, select GitHub Enterprise Server by entering `2`.

1. Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name.

   **Example:**
   
   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   github.example.com
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>

1. Copy/paste the URL into a new browser window/tab.

1. Copy the `webhook_secret=webhook_secret` in the URL.

   **Example output:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name (i.e. github.example.com): github.example.com
   https://github.example.com...webhook_secret=Z1KPOYifctpzOjfphKj_hqRlZbrDOBG9AU7hgj7iPrk...
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>
   
1. Add the `webhook_secret` into the form.

1. Scroll down and click **Create GitHub App**.

1. Take note of the values from your app settings page:

   - Webhook secret from the URL above
   - **App name**
   - **App ID**
   - **Client secret**
   - **Private key**

1. Save these values in a secure location, such as IBM Vault. 

   You are ready to add GitHub as a data source in HCP Vault Radar.

### Set up HCP Vault Radar

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data sources**.

1. Click **Add data source**.

1. Select **HCP Vault Radar Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Enterprise Server**.

1. Under **Select authentication method**, click the dropdown and select **GitHub OAuth** to authenticate with the GitHub App.

1. Select which GitHub App to authenticate with from the **GitHub app** dropdown.

1. Click **Connect to GitHub**.

1. On redirect to GitHub, select which GitHub Org to onboard and the repositories to install on.

1. On successful redirect to **Vault Radar**, click **Next**.

1. Select either **All active repositories** or **Select repositories to monitor**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar agent scan">

### Set up GitHub app authentication

You must create and register a GitHub App before setting up HCP Vault Radar.

1. Download `githubchecks-app.gen.py`.

   ```python
   import logging
   import sys
   from secrets import token_urlsafe
   from urllib.parse import urlencode, urlparse
   
   def init_logger():
       logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
       logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
       h = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
       logger.addHandler(h)
   
       return logger
   
   logger = init_logger()
   
   if __name__ == '__main__':
       print("Please select an option GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server:")
       print("1. GitHub Cloud")
       print("2. GitHub Enterprise Server")
       choice = input("Enter 1 or 2: ")
   
       name = 'HashiCorp Vault Radar Checks App'
       domain = 'github.com'
       webhook_url =  'https://api.hashicorp.cloud/2023-05-01/vault-radar/api/github-apps/events'
       if choice == '1':
           name = input('Enter a unique GitHub app name: ')
       elif choice == '2':
           domain = input('Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name (i.e. github.companyname.com): ')
           domain = urlparse(domain if '//' in domain else f'//{domain}').netloc
           webhook_url = webhook_url + f"?domain={domain}"
       else:
           print("Invalid option")
           sys.exit()
   
       query_params = {
           'name': name,
           'webhook_secret': token_urlsafe(nbytes=32),
           'webhook_url': webhook_url,
           'public': True,
           'webhook_active': True,
           'url': 'https://www.hashicorp.com/',
           'checks': 'write',
           'events[]': ['check_run', 'check_suite', 'pull_request'],
           'pull_requests': 'read',
       }
       query_param_string = urlencode(query_params, doseq=True, safe=':/?=[]')
       
       url = f'https://{domain}/settings/apps/new?{query_param_string}'
       logger.info(url)
   ```

   You can view the source code for this script in the [HashiCorp guides GitHub
   repository](https://github.com/hashicorp-guides/vault-radar-demo/tree/main/github-app).

1. Run the script and follow the prompt.

   ```shell-session
   $ python3 github-checks-app-gen.py
   ```

1. When prompted, select GitHub Enterprise Server by entering `2`.

1. Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name.

   **Example:**
   
   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   github.example.com
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>

1. Copy/paste the URL into a new browser window/tab.

1. Copy the `webhook_secret=webhook_secret` in the URL.

   **Example output:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   Enter your GitHub Enterprise Server domain name (i.e. github.companyname.com): acme.github.com
   https://acme.github.com...webhook_secret=Z1KPOYifctpzOjfphKj_hqRlZbrDOBG9AU7hgj7iPrk...
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>
   
1. Add the `webhook_secret` into the form.

1. Scroll down and click **Create GitHub App**.

1. Take note of the values from your app settings page:

   - Webhook secret from the URL above
   - **App name**
   - **App ID**
   - **Private key**

1. Save these values in a secure location, such as IBM Vault. 

1. [Configure an environment variable](/hcp/docs/vault-radar/agent/deploy) on
   the agent with the value of the private key.

   **Example configuration:** 

   If you run the agent on Kubernetes, add the environment
   variable to the agent deployment manifest.

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   env:
     - name: GH_RADAR_PRIVATE_KEY
       value: `string-of-your-private-key`
   ```
   
   </CodeBlockConfig>

   <Highlight>

   Use the same variable name on all agents.

   </Highlight>

   You are ready to add GitHub as a data source in HCP Vault Radar.

### Set up HCP Vault Radar

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data sources**.

1. Click **Add data source**.

1. Select **Vault Radar Agent Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Enterprise Server**.

1. Enter the path to the environment variable with the personal access token in
   the Enter environment path to your GitHub personal access token field.

   **Example:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   env://GH_RADAR_PRIVATE_KEY
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>

1. Under **Select authentication method**, click the dropdown and select **GitHub OAuth** to authenticate with the GitHub App.

1. Select which GitHub App to authenticate with from the **GitHub app** dropdown.

1. Click **Connect to GitHub**.

1. On redirect to GitHub, select which GitHub Org to onboard and the repositories to install on.

1. On successful redirect to **Vault Radar**, click **Next**.

1. Select either **All active repositories** or **Select repositories to monitor**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## Add a GitHub data source host with PAT authentication

You can add and scan GitHub Cloud or GitHub Enterprise Server using the **HCP
Vault Radar cloud scan** or **HCP Vault Radar agent scan**.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="GitHub Cloud">

Add GitHub Cloud as a data source to scan your GitHub organization's repositories for secrets.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar cloud scan">

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data Source Hosts**.

1. Click **Add data source host**.

1. Select **HCP Vault Radar Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Cloud**.

1. Enter the personal access token in the **Enter your GitHub personal
   access token** field.

1. Click **Next**.

1. Select either **All data sources** or **Select data sources**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar agent scan">

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data Source Hosts**.

1. Click **Add data source host**.

1. Select **Vault Radar Agent Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Cloud**.

1. Enter the path to the environment variable with the personal access token in
   the **Enter environment path to your GitHub personal access token**
   field.
   
   **Example:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   env://GITHUB_TOKEN
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>

1. Click **Next**.

1. Select either **All data sources** or **Select data sources**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

</Tab>
<Tab heading="GitHub Enterprise Server">

Add GitHub Enterprise Server as a data source to scan your GitHub organization's repositories for secrets.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar cloud scan">

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data Source Hosts**.

1. Click **Add data source host**.

1. Select **HCP Vault Radar Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Enterprise Server**.

1. Enter the following information:
   
   - **Enter your GitHub domain name:** Fully qualified domain name of
     your GitHub Enterprise Server.
   - **Enter your GitHub personal access token:** Access token from your GitHub Enterprise
     Server.

1. Click **Next**.

1. Select either **All data sources** or **Select data sources**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="HCP Vault Radar agent scan">

1. Log into the [HCP Portal](https://portal.cloud.hashicorp.com/sign-in) with an
   HCP IAM user that has the HCP owner or admin role.

1. Click **Vault Radar**.

1. Click **Settings**.

1. Click **Data Source Hosts**.

1. Click **Add data source host**.

1. Select **Vault Radar Agent Scan**.

1. Select **GitHub Enterprise Server**.

1. Enter the following information:
   
   - **Enter your GitHub domain name:** Fully qualified domain name of
     your GitHub Enterprise Server.
   - **Enter environment path to your GitHub personal access token:** Access token from your GitHub Enterprise
     Server.
   
   **Example:**

   <CodeBlockConfig hideClipboard>

   ```plaintext
   env://GITHUB_TOKEN
   ```

   </CodeBlockConfig>

1. Click **Next**.

1. Select either **All data sources** or **Select data sources**.

1. Click **Finish**.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

</Tab>
</Tabs>
